title: Grand Rapids, Michigan

infobox.key: • [Mayor](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "List of mayors of Grand Rapids, Michigan")

infobox.value: US: [128th](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") MI: [2nd](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Michigan_(by_population) "List of municipalities in Michigan (by population)")

is a city in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Kent County, Michigan](/wiki/Kent_County,_Michigan "Kent County, Michigan"), United States.[[5]](#cite_note-NACo-5) It is the [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Michigan "List of municipalities in Michigan") in Michigan, with a population of 198,917 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") (estimated at 200,117 in 2024).[[6]](#cite_note-6) The [Grand Rapids metropolitan area](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_metropolitan_area "Grand Rapids metropolitan area"), with over 1.18 million residents, is the [49th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the U.S. Grand Rapids is situated along the [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Michigan) "Grand River (Michigan)") approximately 25 miles (40 km) east of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") and is the economic and cultural hub of [West Michigan](/wiki/West_Michigan "West Michigan").

**Grand Rapids** is a city in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Kent County, Michigan](/wiki/Kent_County,_Michigan "Kent County, Michigan"), United States.[[5]](#cite_note-NACo-5) It is the [second-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Michigan "List of municipalities in Michigan") in Michigan, with a population of 198,917 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") (estimated at 200,117 in 2024).[[6]](#cite_note-6) The [Grand Rapids metropolitan area](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_metropolitan_area "Grand Rapids metropolitan area"), with over 1.18 million residents, is the [49th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the U.S. Grand Rapids is situated along the [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Michigan) "Grand River (Michigan)") approximately 25 miles (40 km) east of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") and is the economic and cultural hub of [West Michigan](/wiki/West_Michigan "West Michigan").

Originally inhabited by the [Hopewell](/wiki/Hopewell_tradition "Hopewell tradition") and later [Odawa](/wiki/Odawa "Odawa") people, the area was settled by European Americans in the early 19th century and incorporated in 1850. Grand Rapids gained prominence in the late 19th Century as the "Furniture City" due to its thriving furniture manufacturing industry, a legacy that continues to influence the region's industrial profile. Its economy is diversified, encompassing healthcare, education, manufacturing, and technology, with major employers such as [Corewell Health](/wiki/Corewell_Health "Corewell Health"), [Meijer](/wiki/Meijer "Meijer"), and [Steelcase](/wiki/Steelcase "Steelcase") anchoring its economic landscape.

Culturally, Grand Rapids is home to several museums, including the [Grand Rapids Art Museum](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Art_Museum "Grand Rapids Art Museum") and [Grand Rapids Public Museum](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Public_Museum "Grand Rapids Public Museum"). The city also hosts the annual [ArtPrize](/wiki/ArtPrize "ArtPrize"), an international art competition, and the [Frederik Meijer Gardens & Sculpture Park](/wiki/Frederik_Meijer_Gardens_%26_Sculpture_Park "Frederik Meijer Gardens & Sculpture Park"), a premier horticultural and artistic destination. As a result of the numerous [craft breweries](/wiki/Craft_beer "Craft beer") in the city, including [Founders Brewing Company](/wiki/Founders_Brewing_Company "Founders Brewing Company"), Grand Rapids is also known as "Beer City USA". Grand Rapids was the [childhood home](/wiki/President_Gerald_R._Ford_Jr._Boyhood_Home "President Gerald R. Ford Jr. Boyhood Home") of U.S. President [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford"), who is buried with his wife [Betty](/wiki/Betty_Ford "Betty Ford") on the grounds of the [Gerald R. Ford Presidential Museum](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_Presidential_Museum "Gerald R. Ford Presidential Museum") in the city.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The city's [Gerald R. Ford International Airport](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_International_Airport "Gerald R. Ford International Airport") and [Gerald R. Ford Freeway](/wiki/Interstate_196 "Interstate 196") are named after him.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Grand Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/History_of_Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "History of Grand Rapids, Michigan")

### 19th century: Native American and European-American settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 19th century: Native American and European-American settlement")]

#### Trading post

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Trading post")]

After the French established territories in Michigan, [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") missionaries and traders traveled down Lake Michigan and its tributaries.[[8]](#cite_note-HISTORYlowell-8)

In 1806, white trader Joseph La Framboise and his [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis "Métis") wife, [Madeline La Framboise](/wiki/Magdelaine_Laframboise "Magdelaine Laframboise"), traveled by canoe from [Mackinac Island](/wiki/Mackinac_Island "Mackinac Island") and established the first trading post in [West Michigan](/wiki/West_Michigan "West Michigan") in present-day Grand Rapids on the banks of the [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Michigan) "Grand River (Michigan)"), near what is now [Ada Township](/wiki/Ada_Township,_Michigan "Ada Township, Michigan"), the junction of the Grand and [Thornapple Rivers](/wiki/Thornapple_River "Thornapple River"). They were French-speaking and [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"). They likely both spoke [Odawa](/wiki/Odawa "Odawa"), Magdelaine's maternal ancestral language. In the fall of 1806, Joseph was fatally stabbed by a member of the [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi") tribe named Nequat. Joseph had been with his family and an entourage of voyageurs traveling between Grand River and Grand Rapids. The Potawatomi man had insisted that Joseph trade liquor with him. When Joseph refused, the man left, only to return at dusk when Joseph, who faithfully performed the ritual of [Angelus](/wiki/Angelus "Angelus") every day at that time, was in prayer. Nequat stabbed the trader, fatally wounding him, leaving Joseph's wife, Magdelaine, a [widow](/wiki/Widow "Widow") at age twenty-four.[[9]](#cite_note-Joseph_LaFramboise:_A_factor_of_tre-9)

The next spring, a delegation from the Potawatomi tribe brought the offender, Nequat, before Magdelaine for her sentence upon him for the death of her husband. It was their tradition for the victim's family to avenge deaths within that tribe. Magdelaine refused to sentence him and, in an act of forgiveness, told the Potawatomi tribe members to let him go and that God would be his judge. Though Magdelaine had forgiven Nequat, the tribe had not. Nequat's body was found stabbed with his own knife the next season.[[9]](#cite_note-Joseph_LaFramboise:_A_factor_of_tre-9)

After Joseph's murder while en route to Grand Rapids, Magdelaine La Framboise carried on the trade business, expanding fur trading posts to the west and north, creating a good reputation among the [American Fur Company](/wiki/American_Fur_Company "American Fur Company"). La Framboise, whose mother was Odawa and father French, later merged her successful operations with the American Fur Company.[[8]](#cite_note-HISTORYlowell-8)

#### Native American settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Native American settlement")]

By 1810, [Chief Noonday](/wiki/Chief_Noonday "Chief Noonday"), or Nowaquakezick, an Odawa chief, established the village of Bock-a-tinck (from *Baawiting*, "at the rapids") on the northwest side of present-day Grand Rapids near Bridge Street with about 500 Odawa, though the population would grow to over 1,000 on occasion.[[10]](#cite_note-HISTORY1881-10)[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11) During the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), Noonday was allied with [Tecumseh](/wiki/Tecumseh "Tecumseh") during the [Battle of the Thames](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Thames "Battle of the Thames"). Tecumseh was killed in this battle, and Noonday inherited his tomahawk and hat.[[12]](#cite_note-MSU-12) A second village existed lower down the river with its center located at the intersection of what is now Watson Street and National Avenue, with Chief Black Skin – known by his native name recorded as Muck-i-ta-oska or Mukatasha (from *Makadewazhe* or *Mkadewzhe*, "Have Black Skin") and was son of Chief Noonday – leading the village.[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11)

#### European settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: European settlement")]

In 1820, General [Lewis Cass](/wiki/Lewis_Cass "Lewis Cass"), who was on his way to negotiate the first [Treaty of Chicago](/wiki/Treaty_of_Chicago#1821_Treaty_of_Chicago "Treaty of Chicago") with a group of 42 men, commissioned [Charles Christopher Trowbridge](/wiki/Charles_Christopher_Trowbridge "Charles Christopher Trowbridge") to establish missions for Native Americans in the Grand River Valley, in hopes of evangelizing them.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11) In 1821, the [Council of Three Fires](/wiki/Council_of_Three_Fires "Council of Three Fires") signed the first [Treaty of Chicago](/wiki/Treaty_of_Chicago#1821_Treaty_of_Chicago "Treaty of Chicago"), ceding to the United States all lands in Michigan Territory south of the Grand River, except for several small reservations, and required a native to prepare land in the area to establish a mission.[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11)[[14]](#cite_note-:5-14) The treaty also included "One hundred thousand dollars to satisfy sundry individuals, in behalf of whom reservations were asked, which the Commissioners refused to grant" of which Joseph La Framboise received 1,000 dollars immediately and 200 dollars a year, for life.[[14]](#cite_note-:5-14) Madeline La Framboise retired the trading post to [Rix Robinson](/wiki/Rix_Robinson "Rix Robinson") in 1821 and returned to Mackinac.[[8]](#cite_note-HISTORYlowell-8) That year, Grand Rapids was described as being the home of an Odawa village of about 50 to 60 huts on the north side of the river near the 5th Ward, with Kewkishkam being the village chief and Chief Noonday being the chief of the Odawa.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)

The first permanent European-American settler in the Grand Rapids area was [Isaac McCoy](/wiki/Isaac_McCoy "Isaac McCoy"), a [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") minister.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13) In 1823, McCoy, Paget, a Frenchman who brought along a Native American pupil, and a government worker traveled to Grand Rapids from Carey Mission near present-day [Niles, Michigan](/wiki/Niles,_Michigan "Niles, Michigan") to arrange a mission they called the "Thomas Mission", though negotiations fell through with the group returning to the Carey Mission for the Potawatomi on the [St. Joseph River](/wiki/St._Joseph_River_(Lake_Michigan) "St. Joseph River (Lake Michigan)").[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11) The government worker stayed into 1824 to establish a blacksmith shop, though the shop was burned down by the Odawa.[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11) Later in May 1824, Baptist missionary Reverend Leonard Slater traveled with two settlers to Grand Rapids to perform missionary work, though the group began to return to the Carey Mission after only three days due to threats.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11) While the group was returning, they encountered Chief Noonday who asked for the group to stay and establish a mission, believing that the Odawa adapting to European customs was the only chance for them to stay in the area.[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11) The winter of 1824 was difficult, with Slater's group having to resupply and return before the spring.[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11)[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13) Chief Noonday, deciding to be an example for the Odawa, chose to be baptized by Slater in the Grand River, though some of his followers believed that this was a wrestling match between the two that Slater won.[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11) Slater then erected the first settler structures in Grand Rapids, a log cabin for himself and a log schoolhouse.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13) In 1825, McCoy returned and established a missionary station.[[15]](#cite_note-15) He represented the settlers who began arriving from Ohio, New York and New England, the Yankee states of the Northern Tier.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Grand_Rapids_1831.jpg/250px-Grand_Rapids_1831.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Rapids_1831.jpg)

A sketch of Grand Rapids in 1831. The collection of houses across the river on its west side is the Baptist mission. The three buildings in the middle right are Louis Campau's trading post.

Shortly after, Detroit-born [Louis Campau](/wiki/Louis_Campau "Louis Campau"), known as the official founder of Grand Rapids, was convinced by fur trader William Brewster, who was in a rivalry with the American Fur Company, to travel to Grand Rapids and establish trade there.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13) In 1826, Campau built his cabin, trading post, and blacksmith shop on the south bank of the Grand River near the rapids, stating the Native Americans in the area were "friendly and peaceable".[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13) Campau returned to Detroit, then returned a year later with his wife and $5,000 of trade goods to trade with the Odawa and Ojibwa, with the only currency being fur.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13) Campau's younger brother Touissant would often assist him with trade and other tasks at hand.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)

[Lucius Lyon](/wiki/Lucius_Lyon "Lucius Lyon"), a Yankee Protestant who would later become a rival to Campau, was contracted by the federal government to survey the Grand River Valley in the fall of 1830 and in the first quarter of 1831. The federal survey of the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory") reached the Grand River, with Lyon using a [surveyor's compass](/wiki/Surveyor%27s_compass "Surveyor's compass") and [chain](/wiki/Surveyor%27s_chain "Surveyor's chain") to set the boundaries for Kent County, named after prominent New York jurist [James Kent](/wiki/James_Kent_(jurist) "James Kent (jurist)").[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11) In 1833, a land office was established in [White Pigeon, Michigan](/wiki/White_Pigeon,_Michigan "White Pigeon, Michigan"), with Campau and fellow settler Luther Lincoln seeking land in the Grand River valley.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13) Lincoln purchased land in what is now known as [Grandville](/wiki/Grandville,_Michigan "Grandville, Michigan"), while Campau became perhaps the most important settler when he bought 72 acres (291,000 m2) from the federal government for $90 and named his tract Grand Rapids. Over time, it developed as today's main downtown business district.[[16]](#cite_note-Grand_Rapids_Historical_Perspective-16) In the spring of 1833, Campau sold to Joel Guild, who traveled from New York, a plot of land for $25.00, with Guild building the first frame structure in Grand Rapids, which is now where [McKay Tower](/wiki/McKay_Tower "McKay Tower") stands.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)[[17]](#cite_note-Mlive1-17) Guild later became the postmaster, with mail at the time being delivered monthly from the [Gull Lake, Michigan](/wiki/Gull_Lake,_Michigan "Gull Lake, Michigan") to Grand Rapids.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13) Grand Rapids in 1833 was only a few acres of land cleared on each side of the Grand River, with oak trees planted in light, sandy soil standing between what is now Lyon Street and Fulton Street.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Rathbun_House_1870.jpg/250px-Rathbun_House_1870.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rathbun_House_1870.jpg)

The large framed building constructed by Campau in 1834, seen in this image converted into part of the Rathbun House.

By 1834, the settlement had become more organized. Rev. Turner had established a school on the east side of the river, with children on the west side of the river being brought to school every morning by a Native American on a canoe who would shuttle them across the river. Multiple events happened at Guild's frame structure, including the first marriage in the city, one that involved his daughter Harriet Guild and Barney Burton, as well as the first town meeting that had nine voters. It was also this year Campau began constructing his own frame building—the largest at the time—near present-day Rosa Parks Circle.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)

In 1835, many settlers arrived in the area with the population growing to about 50 people, including its first doctor, Dr. Wilson, who was supplied with equipment from Campau.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13) [Lucius Lyon](/wiki/Lucius_Lyon "Lucius Lyon"), using his knowledge from surveying the area, returned to Grand Rapids to purchase the rest of the prime land and called his plot the Village of Kent.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)[[11]](#cite_note-WILD-11) When Lyon and his partner N. O. Sergeant returned after their purchase, they arrived along with a posse of men carrying shovels and picks, intending to build a [mill race](/wiki/Mill_race "Mill race"). The group arrived to the music of a bugle which startled the settlement, with Chief Noonday offering Campau assistance to drive back Lyon's posse believing they were invaders. Also that year, Rev. Andrew Vizoisky, a Hungarian native educated in Catholic institutions in Austria, arrived, presiding over the Catholic mission in the area until his death in 1852.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)

That year, Campau, Rix Robinson, Rev. Slater, and the husband of Chief Noonday's daughter, Meccissininni, traveled to Washington, D.C. to speak about the purchase of Odawa land on the west side of the river with President [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson").[[10]](#cite_note-HISTORY1881-10) Jackson was originally unimpressed with Meccissininni, though Meccissininni, who often acquired white customs, asked Jackson for a similar suit to the one the president was wearing. While later wearing his suit that was made similar to Jackson's, Meccissininni also unknowingly imitated Jackson's hat, placing a piece of weed in it, which impressed Jackson since it symbolized mourning the death of his wife.[[10]](#cite_note-HISTORY1881-10)

[John Ball](/wiki/John_Ball_(pioneer) "John Ball (pioneer)"), representing a group of New York land speculators, bypassed Detroit for a better deal in Grand Rapids traveling to the settlement in 1836. Ball declared the Grand River valley "the promised land, or at least the most promising one for my operations".[[18]](#cite_note-18) That year, the first steamboat was constructed on the Grand River named the *Gov. Mason*, though the ship wrecked two years later in Muskegon.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13) [Yankee](/wiki/Yankee "Yankee") migrants (primarily English-speaking settlers) and others began migrating from New York and [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") through the 1830s. Ancestors of these people included not only English colonists but people of mixed ethnic Dutch, [Mohawk](/wiki/Mohawk_people "Mohawk people"), French Canadian, and [French Huguenot](/wiki/French_Huguenot "French Huguenot") descent from the colonial period in New York. However, after 1837, the area saw poor times, with many of the French returning to their places of origin, with poverty hitting the area for the next few years.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)

The first Grand Rapids newspaper, *The Grand River Times*, was printed on April 18, 1837, describing the village's attributes, stating:[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)

> Though young in its improvements, the site of this village has long been known and esteemed for its natural advantages. It was here that the Indian traders long since made their great depot.

*The Grand River Times* continued, saying the village had grown quickly from a few French families to about 1,200 residents, the Grand River was "one of the most important and delightful to be found in the country," and described the changing Native American culture in the area.[[13]](#cite_note-HISTORY1870-13)

#### Incorporation and cityhood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Incorporation and cityhood")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/MI_Grand_Rapids_1868.jpg/250px-MI_Grand_Rapids_1868.jpg)](/wiki/File:MI_Grand_Rapids_1868.jpg)

An 1868 [pictorial map](/wiki/Pictorial_map "Pictorial map") of Grand Rapids

By 1838, the settlement incorporated as a village, and covered approximately .75 square miles (1.9 km2).[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19)

An outcropping of [gypsum](/wiki/Gypsum "Gypsum"), where [Plaster Creek](/wiki/Plaster_Creek "Plaster Creek") enters the Grand River, was known to the Native American inhabitants of the area. Pioneer geologist [Douglass Houghton](/wiki/Douglass_Houghton "Douglass Houghton") commented on this find in 1838.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Settlers began to mine this outcrop in 1841, initially in [open cast mines](/wiki/Open-pit_mining "Open-pit mining"), but later underground mines as well. Gypsum was ground locally for use as a soil amendment known as "land plaster."

The first formal census in 1845 recorded a population of 1,510[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22) and an area of 4 square miles (10 km2).[[22]](#cite_note-:1-22) The city of Grand Rapids was incorporated April 2, 1850.[[23]](#cite_note-23) It was officially established on May 2, 1850, when the village of Grand Rapids voted to accept the proposed city charter. The population at the time was 2,686. By 1857, the city of Grand Rapids' area totaled 10.5 square miles (27 km2).[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19) Through the 1850s, the land containing forty-six [Indian mounds](/wiki/Indian_mounds "Indian mounds") located on the west side between Bridge Street and the Grand River to the south were sold by the United States government, with the mounds being destroyed to fill low-lying land in the area while the Native American artifacts contained within were taken or sold to museums, including the [Grand Rapids Public Museum](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Public_Museum "Grand Rapids Public Museum").[[24]](#cite_note-24) In October 1870, Grand Rapids became a desired location for immigrants, with about 120 Swedes arriving in the United States to travel and create a "colony" in the area in one week.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

During the second half of the nineteenth century, the city became a major lumbering center, processing timber harvested in the region. Logs were floated down the Grand River to be milled in the city and shipped via the Great Lakes. The city became a center of fine wood products as well. By the end of the century, it was established as the premier furniture-manufacturing city of the United States.[[26]](#cite_note-pubmuseum-26) It was the [Centennial Exposition](/wiki/Centennial_Exposition "Centennial Exposition") of 1876 in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") that brought attention to Grand Rapids' furniture on the national stage, providing a new growing industry to help the city recover from the [Panic of 1873](/wiki/Panic_of_1873 "Panic of 1873").[[27]](#cite_note-:03-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) In 1880, the country's first hydro-electric generator was put to use on the city's west side.[[29]](#cite_note-hydro-29)

### 20th century: Furniture City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 20th century: Furniture City")]

Due to its flourishing furniture industry, Grand Rapids began being recognized as "Furniture City". Grand Rapids was also an early center for the [automobile industry](/wiki/Automobile_industry "Automobile industry"), as the [Austin Automobile Company](/wiki/Austin_Automobile_Company "Austin Automobile Company") operated there from 1901 until 1921.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Grand_Rapids_c1915_6a13439u.jpg/960px-Grand_Rapids_c1915_6a13439u.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Rapids_c1915_6a13439u.jpg "A 1915 panorama, when the furniture industry peaked before the Great Depression.")

A 1915 panorama, when the furniture industry peaked before the Great Depression.

Furniture companies included the William A. Berkey Company and its successors, [Baker Furniture Company](/w/index.php?title=Baker_Furniture_Company&action=edit&redlink=1 "Baker Furniture Company (page does not exist)"), Williams-Kimp, and [Widdicomb Furniture Company](/wiki/Widdicomb_Furniture_Company "Widdicomb Furniture Company").[[30]](#cite_note-30) The furniture industry began to grow significantly into the twentieth century; in 1870 there were eight factories employing 280 workers and by 1911, [Old National Bank](/wiki/Old_National_Bank "Old National Bank") wrote that about 8,500 were employed by forty-seven factories.[[27]](#cite_note-:03-27)[[31]](#cite_note-VIVA-31) At least a third of the workers in Grand Rapids were employed by furniture companies.[[27]](#cite_note-:03-27) *The Grand Rapids Furniture Record* was the trade paper for the city's industry. Its industries provided jobs for many new immigrants from Europe in the late 19th and early 20th century, and a Polish neighborhood developed on the west side of the city.

#### Strike of 1911

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Strike of 1911")]

Main article: [1911 Grand Rapids furniture workers strike](/wiki/1911_Grand_Rapids_furniture_workers_strike "1911 Grand Rapids furniture workers strike")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Grand_Rapids_furniture_strike_-_riot_police.jpg/250px-Grand_Rapids_furniture_strike_-_riot_police.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Rapids_furniture_strike_-_riot_police.jpg)

The [riot police](/wiki/Riot_police "Riot police") who responded to the [1911 Grand Rapids furniture workers strike](/wiki/1911_Grand_Rapids_furniture_workers_strike "1911 Grand Rapids furniture workers strike")

By the early twentieth century, the quality of furniture produced in Grand Rapids was renowned throughout the furniture industry, mainly due to the skill of its workers.[[31]](#cite_note-VIVA-31) Government reports in 1907 revealed that while Grand Rapids led the industry in product output, its furniture workers were paid lower wages than in other areas.[[31]](#cite_note-VIVA-31) After a minor dispute, workers were inspired to form labor unions; workers requested furniture companies to increase wages, fewer working hours, the creation of [collective bargaining](/wiki/Collective_bargaining "Collective bargaining") and the institution of a [minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage "Minimum wage") to replace [piece work](/wiki/Piece_work "Piece work").[[27]](#cite_note-:03-27)[[31]](#cite_note-VIVA-31) The furniture businesses refused to respond with unions as they believed that any meeting represented recognition of unions.[[27]](#cite_note-:03-27)[[31]](#cite_note-VIVA-31)

Workers in Grand Rapids then began a four month long [general strike](/wiki/General_strike "General strike") on April 19, 1911.[[27]](#cite_note-:03-27)[[32]](#cite_note-MLIVE12-32) Much of the public, the mayor, the press and the Catholic diocese supported the strike, believing that the unwillingness of business leaders to negotiate was unjust. Skilled and unskilled factory labor was mainly Dutch (60 percent) and Polish (25 percent), primarily immigrants. According to the 1911 Immigration Commission report, the Dutch had an average of 8 percent higher wages than the Poles even when they did the same work. The pay difference was based on seniority and not ethnicity, but given that the Dutch had arrived earlier, seniority was linked to ethnicity.[[27]](#cite_note-:03-27)[[31]](#cite_note-VIVA-31) Ultimately, the Christian Reformed Church – where the majority of Dutch striking workers congregated – and the [Fountain Street Church](/wiki/Fountain_Street_Church "Fountain Street Church") – led opposition to the strike, which resulted in its end on August 19, 1911.[[27]](#cite_note-:03-27)[[32]](#cite_note-MLIVE12-32)

The strike spurred substantial changes to the governmental and labor structure of the city.[[32]](#cite_note-MLIVE12-32) With businesses upset with Mayor Ellis for supporting the strike lobbied for the city to change from a twelve-ward government – which more accurately represented the city's ethnic groups – to a smaller three ward system that placed more power into the demands of Dutch citizens, the city's largest demographic.[[33]](#cite_note-:06-33)[[32]](#cite_note-MLIVE12-32) Some workers who participated in the strike were [blacklisted](/wiki/Blacklisted "Blacklisted") by companies and thousands of dissatisfied furniture workers emigrated to higher paying regions.[[27]](#cite_note-:03-27)[[31]](#cite_note-VIVA-31)

#### Move to retail and suburbanization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Move to retail and suburbanization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Grand_Rapids_Monroe_Center_1965.jpg/250px-Grand_Rapids_Monroe_Center_1965.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Rapids_Monroe_Center_1965.jpg)

Monroe Center in January 1965, with [Sears](/wiki/Sears "Sears"), [S. S. Kresge Company](/wiki/S._S._Kresge_Company "S. S. Kresge Company"), [Steketee's](/wiki/Steketee%27s "Steketee's") and Wurzburg's visible

Shifting from its furniture-centric industry, downtown Grand Rapids temporarily became a retail destination for the region, hosting four department stores: Herpolsheimer's ([Lazarus](/wiki/Lazarus_(department_store) "Lazarus (department store)")), [Jacobson's](/wiki/Jacobson%27s "Jacobson's"), [Steketee's](/wiki/Steketee%27s "Steketee's") (founded in 1862), and Wurzburg's. In 1945, Grand Rapids became the first city in the United States to add [fluoride](/wiki/Fluoridation "Fluoridation") to its drinking water. National home furnishing conferences were held in Grand Rapids for about seventy-five years, concluding in the 1960s. By that time, the furniture-making industry had largely shifted to [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

As with many older cities in the United States, retail in the city suffered as the population moved to suburbs in the postwar era, enabled in part by federal subsidies for highway construction. The Grand Rapids suburbs began to develop rapidly in the 1950s and 1960s. For example, [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming,_Michigan "Wyoming, Michigan") saw rapid growth following the opening of retail outlets such as [Rogers Plaza](/wiki/Rogers_Plaza "Rogers Plaza") and Wyoming Village Mall on [28th Street](/wiki/M-11_(Michigan_highway) "M-11 (Michigan highway)"), with some developments built so quickly that they were finished without functioning utilities.[[35]](#cite_note-CVDS77-35)

#### Civic renewal projects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Civic renewal projects")]

Following [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford")'s loss in the [1976 United States presidential election](/wiki/1976_United_States_presidential_election "1976 United States presidential election"), his security team rejected a welcoming parade initially, saying that the city was too dangerous, though agreed to hold an event after all local security bodies assisted.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) This incident upset local leaders and entrepreneurs, who went on to plan civic renewal projects in the area.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) One of the first renewal projects was performed by [Amway](/wiki/Amway "Amway") co-founder [Jay Van Andel](/wiki/Jay_Van_Andel "Jay Van Andel"), who funded the renovation of the [Pantlind Hotel](/wiki/Pantlind_Hotel "Pantlind Hotel") and construction of [Amway Grand Plaza Hotel](/wiki/Amway_Grand_Plaza_Hotel "Amway Grand Plaza Hotel") tower, which was completed in 1981.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36)

In 1983, the Grand Rapids Chamber of Commerce, using funding from Van Andel, created the Right Place.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) The Right Place evolved from a committee of the chamber and later became a provate entity tasked with created business opportuinites and retaining talent in West Michigan.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) Local leaders including Van Andel, fellow Amway co-founder [Richard DeVos](/wiki/Richard_DeVos "Richard DeVos"), the founding families of Hunting, Pew, and Wege of [Steelcase](/wiki/Steelcase "Steelcase"), the founder of [Universal Forest Products](/wiki/Universal_Forest_Products "Universal Forest Products"), [Peter Secchia](/wiki/Peter_Secchia "Peter Secchia") and the Meijer family of the supermarket chain [Meijer](/wiki/Meijer "Meijer") began philanthropic work in the city from the mid-1980s and into the 1990s, providing 25% to 50% of funds to large local developments.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) In 1991, the Grand Vision Committee, which later became the Grand Action Committee, began to draft plans for large civic projects and collected funds, with the Right Place organizing the projects with regional economic goals.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) One of the largest projects of Grand Action was the [Van Andel Arena](/wiki/Van_Andel_Arena "Van Andel Arena"), which was completed in 1996.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36)

### 21st century: Brewing and medical industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 21st century: Brewing and medical industry")]

#### Medical Mile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Medical Mile")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Van_Andel_Institute.jpg/250px-Van_Andel_Institute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Van_Andel_Institute.jpg)

The [Van Andel Institute](/wiki/Van_Andel_Institute "Van Andel Institute"), which led to the development of the [Grand Rapids Medical Mile](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Medical_Mile "Grand Rapids Medical Mile")

The creation of the [Grand Rapids Medical Mile](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Medical_Mile "Grand Rapids Medical Mile") was spurred by the construction of the [Van Andel Institute](/wiki/Van_Andel_Institute "Van Andel Institute"), a biomedical research facility which opened in 2000.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) The Van Andel Institute was provided more than $1 billion to find world-class researchers, with over 400 researchers being located at its facilities.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) In 2003, the West Michigan Science and Technology Initiative was created, helping to bring additional [Spectrum Health](/wiki/Spectrum_Health "Spectrum Health") and [Grand Valley State University](/wiki/Grand_Valley_State_University "Grand Valley State University")facilities into the area.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) The initiative also assisted local manufacturing firms to diversify their products, moving some of their focus from automotive and food supplies into medical device production.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) By 2008, the [Michigan State University College of Human Medicine](/wiki/Michigan_State_University_College_of_Human_Medicine "Michigan State University College of Human Medicine") moved into its new building at the Secchia Center on the Medical Mile.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36)

#### Great Recession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Great Recession")]

Manufacturing primarily comprises the majority of Grand Rapids' economy, which made it vulnerable to the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") in the late 2000s.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In January 2011, *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")* listed Grand Rapids on its list of "America's Dying Cities", which prompted strong negative feedback amongst citizens of the city.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Locals responded to the designation in mid-2011, creating a [lip dub](/wiki/Lip_dub "Lip dub") video to the [Don McLean](/wiki/Don_McLean "Don McLean") song [*American Pie*](/wiki/American_Pie_(song) "American Pie (song)") which showcased various attractions and organizations in the city, featuring thousands of people and being viewed by millions.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

With the city's past investment into the medical industry, Grand Rapids was able to quickly recover from the recession.[[36]](#cite_note-:02-36) In 2012, Grand Rapids was listed as a metropolitan area that experienced the best economic recovery following the Great Recession according to the [Brookings Institution](/wiki/Brookings_Institution "Brookings Institution").[[40]](#cite_note-40) By 2013, Grand Rapids was removed from the list of "dying cities".[[41]](#cite_note-41)

#### Beer City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Beer City")]

In the 2010s, [craft beer](/wiki/Craft_beer "Craft beer") began to grow in popularity in the United States.[[42]](#cite_note-:6-42) Grand Rapids tied [Asheville, North Carolina](/wiki/Asheville,_North_Carolina "Asheville, North Carolina") in 2012 in being named "Beer City USA" in an online poll, winning the title outright in 2013 and eventually having more than 35 local breweries over 100 breweries and cider makers located in the region.[[43]](#cite_note-:7-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) Breweries such as [Founders Brewing Company](/wiki/Founders_Brewing_Company "Founders Brewing Company"), [HopCat](/wiki/HopCat "HopCat"), Vivant Brewing, Harmony Brewing, Perrin Brewing and others led the craft beer scene in the Grand Rapids area at the time.[[43]](#cite_note-:7-43) By 2019, [Craft beer tourism](/wiki/Craft_beer_tourism "Craft beer tourism") resulted with a $38.5 million annual economic impact.[[43]](#cite_note-:7-43) Following [the legalization of non-medical cannabis in Michigan](/wiki/Legalization_of_non-medical_cannabis_in_the_United_States#Legalization_timeline "Legalization of non-medical cannabis in the United States") and the beginning of licensed sales in 2019, the popularity of craft beer began to decline.[[42]](#cite_note-:6-42) Following the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") into the 2020s, production costs and financial difficulties facing consumers resulted with further decreases in craft beer consumption.[[42]](#cite_note-:6-42) The health of the retail sector in Grand Rapids has continued to remain strong, with commercial office vacancy rates remaining lower than the nation average as of 2025.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### Topography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Topography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Grand_Rapids_by_Sentinel-2.jpg/250px-Grand_Rapids_by_Sentinel-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Rapids_by_Sentinel-2.jpg)

The [Grand Rapids metropolitan area](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_metropolitan_area "Grand Rapids metropolitan area") taken by the [Sentinel-2](/wiki/Sentinel-2 "Sentinel-2") satellite in June 2022.

Grand Rapids developed on the banks of the [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Michigan) "Grand River (Michigan)"), where there was once a set of rapids, at an altitude of 610 feet (186 m) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level"). Ships could navigate on the river up to this fall line, stopping because of the rapids. The river valley is flat and narrow, surrounded by steep hills and bluffs. The terrain becomes more rolling hills away from the river. The countryside surrounding the metropolitan area consists of mixed forest and farmland, with large areas of orchards to the northwest. It is approximately 25 mi (40 km) east of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). The state capital of [Lansing](/wiki/Lansing,_Michigan "Lansing, Michigan") lies about 70 mi (110 km) to the east-by-southeast, and [Kalamazoo](/wiki/Kalamazoo,_Michigan "Kalamazoo, Michigan") is about 50 mi (80 km) to the south.

Grand Rapids is divided into four [quadrants](/wiki/Address_(geography)#Quadrants "Address (geography)"), which form a part of mailing addresses in Kent County. The quadrants are NE (northeast), NW (northwest), SE (southeast), and SW (southwest). Fulton Street serves as the north–south dividing line, while Division Avenue serves as the east–west dividing line separating these quadrants.

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 45.27 square miles (117.25 km2), of which, 44.40 square miles (115.00 km2) of it is land and 0.87 square miles (2.25 km2) is water.[[47]](#cite_note-Gazetteer_files-47)

Grand Rapids has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfa*),[[49]](#cite_note-49) with very warm and humid summers, cold and snowy winters, and short and mild springs and autumns.

Even though it is in the middle of the continent, the city experiences some maritime effects due to its location east of Lake Michigan, including a high number of cloudy days during the late fall and winter, delayed heating in the spring, delayed cooling in fall, somewhat moderated temperatures during winter and [lake effect snow](/wiki/Lake_effect_snow "Lake effect snow"). The city averages 75.6 in (192 cm) of snow a year, making it one of the snowiest major cities in the United States.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The area often receives quick and sudden [lake effect snowstorms](/wiki/Lake_effect_snow "Lake effect snow"), producing significant amounts of snowfall.

The months of March, April, October and November are transitional months and the weather can vary. March has experienced a record high of 87 °F (31 °C) and record low of −13 °F (−25 °C). The average last frost date in spring is May 1, and the average first frost in fall is October 11, giving the area a growing season of 162 days.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The city is in plant hardiness zone 6a, while outlying areas are 5b. Some far western suburbs closer to the insulating effect of Lake Michigan are in zone 6b.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Summers are warm or hot, and [heat waves](/wiki/Heat_wave "Heat wave") and [severe weather](/wiki/Severe_weather "Severe weather") outbreaks are common during a typical summer.

The average temperature of the area is 49 °F (9 °C). The highest temperature in the area was recorded on July 13, 1936, at 108 °F (42 °C), and the lowest was recorded on February 13–14, 1899, at −24 °F (−31 °C).[[53]](#cite_note-53) During an average year, sunshine occurs in 46% of the daylight hours. On 138 nights, the temperature dips to below 32 °F (0 °C). On average, 9.2 days a year have temperatures that meet or exceed the 90 °F (32 °C) mark, and 5.6 days a year have lows that are 0 °F (−18 °C) or colder.

The coldest maximum temperature on record was −6 °F (−21 °C) in 1899, whereas the most recent subzero Fahrenheit daily maximum was −2 °F (−19 °C) in 1994.[[54]](#cite_note-GrandRapidsNOAA-54) During the reference period of 1991 to 2020, the coldest daily maximum on average was 11 °F (−12 °C).[[54]](#cite_note-GrandRapidsNOAA-54) Summer nights influenced by the lake can be hot and muggy on occasion. The warmest night on record was 82 °F (28 °C) in 1902 and lows above 72 °F (22 °C) have been measured in every month between April and October.[[54]](#cite_note-GrandRapidsNOAA-54) On average, the warmest low of the year stood at 74 °F (23 °C) for the 1991–2020 normals.[[54]](#cite_note-GrandRapidsNOAA-54)

The most recent record set was the February record high of 73 °F (23 °C), which was recorded on February 27, 2024.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In April 1956, the western and northern portions of the city and its suburbs were hit by a [violent tornado](/wiki/April_1956_tornado_outbreak "April 1956 tornado outbreak") which locally produced [F5 damage](/wiki/Fujita_scale "Fujita scale") and killed 18 people.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

With the Grand River flowing through the center of Grand Rapids, the city has been prone to floods. From March 25 to 29, 1904, more than one-half of the entire populated portion of the city lying on the west side of the river was completely underwater, over 2,500 houses, affecting 14,000 persons, being completely surrounded. On March 28, the river registered at 19.6 feet (6.0 m), more than two feet (0.61 m) above its highest previous mark.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

More than one-hundred years later, the [2013 Grand Rapids flood](/wiki/2013_Grand_Rapids_flood "2013 Grand Rapids flood") occurred from April 12 to 25, 2013, with the river cresting at 21.85 feet (6.66 m) on the 21st, causing thousands of residents to evacuate their homes and over $10 million in damage.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

See also: [List of tallest buildings in Grand Rapids](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Grand_Rapids "List of tallest buildings in Grand Rapids") and [List of nature centers in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_nature_centers_in_Michigan "List of nature centers in Michigan")

The city skyline shows the [Amway Grand Plaza Hotel](/wiki/Amway_Grand_Plaza_Hotel "Amway Grand Plaza Hotel"), formerly the Pantlind, which reopened in 1981 after extensive renovations by Marvin DeWinter & Associates. This work included the addition of a 29–story glass tower offering panoramic views of the city, river and surrounding area. The Pantlind Hotel's original architects, Warren & Wetmore, were inspired by the work of the Scottish neoclassical architect [Robert Adam](/wiki/Robert_Adam "Robert Adam"). In its prime, the hotel was rated as one of the top ten hotels in the US. The hotel features several restaurants well known in Grand Rapids. The hotel is owned by Amway Hotel Collection, a subsidiary of Amway's holding company [Alticor](/wiki/Alticor "Alticor").[[62]](#cite_note-63)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Grand_Rapids%2C_MI_skyline_2021.jpg/960px-Grand_Rapids%2C_MI_skyline_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Rapids,_MI_skyline_2021.jpg "The skyline of Grand Rapids as seen in August 2021")

The skyline of Grand Rapids as seen in August 2021

Other prominent large buildings include the [JW Marriott Grand Rapids](/wiki/JW_Marriott_Grand_Rapids "JW Marriott Grand Rapids"), the first JW Marriott Hotel in the Midwest. It is themed from cityscapes of Grand Rapids' sister cities: Omihachiman, Japan; Bielsko-Biała, Poland; Perugia, Italy; Ga District, Ghana; and Zapopan, Mexico. When the hotel opened, Amway Hotel corporation hired photographer Dan Watts to travel to each of the sister cities and photograph them for the property. Each floor of the hotel features photography from one of the cities, which is unique to that floor. Cityscapes of these five cities are alternated in order, up the 23 floors.

The city's tallest building is the [River House Condominiums](/wiki/River_House_Condominiums "River House Condominiums"), a 34-story (123.8 m) condominium tower completed in 2008 that stands as the tallest all-residential building in the state of Michigan.[[63]](#cite_note-emporiscorp-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Barn_at_Blandford_Nature_Center_in_Springtime.jpg/250px-Barn_at_Blandford_Nature_Center_in_Springtime.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barn_at_Blandford_Nature_Center_in_Springtime.jpg)

A barn belonging to the Blandford School at the Blandford Nature Center in early springtime

Grand Rapids is also home to two large urban nature centers. The Calvin Ecosystem Preserve and Native Gardens, operated by [Calvin University](/wiki/Calvin_University "Calvin University") on the city's southeast side, is 104 acres (42 ha). It is home to over 44 acres (18 ha) of public-access nature trails, a 60-acre (24 ha), restricted-access wildlife preserve, as well as the Bunker Interpretive Center, which hosts university classes and educational programs for the wider community.[[64]](#cite_note-65) The Blandford Nature Center, located on the city's northwest side, opened in 1968 and contains extensive nature trails, an animal hospital, and a "heritage village" made up of several well-preserved 19th-century buildings, including a log cabin, schoolhouse, and barn.[[65]](#cite_note-66) The nature center is also home to Blandford School, a highly selective environmental education program for sixth graders from the metropolitan region, which is run by [Grand Rapids Public Schools](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Public_Schools "Grand Rapids Public Schools") and serves as a feeder school for [City High-Middle School](/wiki/City_High-Middle_School "City High-Middle School"). At 264 acres (107 ha), Blandford is one of the largest urban nature centers in the United States.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the 2010 census,[[74]](#cite_note-wwwcensusgov-75) there were 188,036 people, 72,126 households, and 41,015 families residing in the city. The population density was 4,235.1 inhabitants per square mile (1,635.2/km2). There were 80,619 housing units at an average density of 1,815.7 per square mile (701.0/km2). The city's racial makeup was 64.6% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)") (59.0% [Non-Hispanic White](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_White "Non-Hispanic White")[[75]](#cite_note-76)), 20.9% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.7% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 1.9% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.1% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), 7.7% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), and 4.2% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") residents of any race were 15.6% of the population.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

Of the 72,126 households, 31.1% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 35.5% were married couples living together, 16.4% had a female householder with no husband present, 5.0% had a male householder with no wife present, and 43.1% were non-families. 32.3% of all households were made up of individuals, and 10.1% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.49 and the average family size was 3.20.

The median age in the city was 30.8 years. 24.7% of residents were under the age of 18; 14.5% were between the ages of 18 and 24; 28.6% were from 25 to 44; 21.2% were from 45 to 64; and 11.1% were 65 years of age or older. The city's gender makeup was 48.7% male and 51.3% female.

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

There were 73,217 households, of which 32.0% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 40.3% were married couples living together, 15.8% had a female householder with no husband present, and 39.4% were non-families. 30.8% of all households were made up of individuals, and 10.0% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.57 and the average family size was 3.24.

In the city, the age distribution shows 27.0% under the age of 18, 13.1% from 18 to 24, 31.5% from 25 to 44, 16.7% from 45 to 64, and 11.6% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 30 years. For every 100 females, there were 95.8 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 92.5 males.

The city's median household income was $37,224, and the median family income was $44,224. Males had a median income of $33,050 versus $26,382 for females. The city's per capita income was $17,661. 15.7% of the population and 11.9% of families were below the poverty line. Out of the total people living in poverty, 19.4% are under the age of 18 and 10.4% are 65 or older.

### Ethnicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Ethnicity")]

According to a 2007 American Community Survey, the largest ancestry groups in Grand Rapids reported (not including "American") were those of [German](/wiki/German_American "German American") (23.4% of the population), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_American "Dutch American") (21.2%), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") (11.4%), [English](/wiki/English_American "English American") (10.8%), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_American "Polish American") (6.5%), and [French](/wiki/French_American "French American") (4.1%) heritage.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

After the [Fall of Saigon](/wiki/Fall_of_Saigon "Fall of Saigon"), Grand Rapids welcomed thousands of Vietnamese refugees. Local nonprofits helped them settle throughout West Michigan. Special attention was paid to Grand Rapids because of President [Gerald R. Ford's](/wiki/Gerald_R_Ford "Gerald R Ford") Grand Rapids roots.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

In recent decades, Grand Rapids and its suburban areas have seen their Latino communities grow. Between 2000 and 2010 the Latino population in Grand Rapids grew from 25,818 to 29,261, increasing over 13% in a decade.[[79]](#cite_note-80)

Into the 21st century, the African American population of Grand Rapids continually declined.[[80]](#cite_note-:3-81) In 2022, *The Grand Rapids Press* reported that the population of African Americans in the city declined 4% over the decade, with the newspaper writing that [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification"), increasing rent, [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl") into the neighboring cities of Kentwood and Wyoming—which experienced increased African American population growth—and [New Great Migration](/wiki/New_Great_Migration "New Great Migration") trends contributed to the loss of black residents.[[80]](#cite_note-:3-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82) The decline of African American residents occurred primarily in the northeast and southeast areas of the city.[[82]](#cite_note-83)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/ORDINATION2012_455.JPG/250px-ORDINATION2012_455.JPG)](/wiki/File:ORDINATION2012_455.JPG)

[Cathedral of Saint Andrew](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Saint_Andrew_(Grand_Rapids,_Michigan) "Cathedral of Saint Andrew (Grand Rapids, Michigan)")

The [Christian Reformed Church in North America](/wiki/Christian_Reformed_Church_in_North_America "Christian Reformed Church in North America") has a large following in Grand Rapids, and its denominational offices are located here.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Temple_Emanuel_%28Grand_Rapids%2C_Michigan%29.jpg/250px-Temple_Emanuel_%28Grand_Rapids%2C_Michigan%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Temple_Emanuel_(Grand_Rapids,_Michigan).jpg)

[Temple Emanuel](/wiki/Temple_Emanuel_(Grand_Rapids,_Michigan) "Temple Emanuel (Grand Rapids, Michigan)") in 2019

The [Reform Judaism](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") congregation of [Temple Emanuel](/wiki/Temple_Emanuel_(Grand_Rapids,_Michigan) "Temple Emanuel (Grand Rapids, Michigan)") was founded in 1857 and the fifth oldest Reform congregation in the United States.[[84]](#cite_note-85) The congregation built its first synagogue in 1882 on the corner of Fountain and Ransom Streets. The current location was constructed in 1952.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

Grand Rapids is home to the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Grand Rapids](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Grand_Rapids "Roman Catholic Diocese of Grand Rapids"), which was created on May 19, 1882, by [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII"). The Diocese serves 176,098 Catholics in [West Michigan](/wiki/West_Michigan "West Michigan"), 102 parishes, and five high schools: [Catholic Central High School](/wiki/Catholic_Central_High_School_(Grand_Rapids,_Michigan) "Catholic Central High School (Grand Rapids, Michigan)"), Grand Rapids; [Muskegon Catholic Central High School](/wiki/Muskegon_Catholic_Central_High_School "Muskegon Catholic Central High School"), Muskegon; [St. Patrick High School](/wiki/St._Patrick_High_School_(Portland,_Michigan) "St. Patrick High School (Portland, Michigan)"), Portland; Sacred Heart Academy, Grand Rapids; and [West Catholic High School](/wiki/West_Catholic_High_School "West Catholic High School"), Grand Rapids.[[86]](#cite_note-facts-87) [David John Walkowiak](/wiki/David_John_Walkowiak "David John Walkowiak") is the [Bishop of Grand Rapids](/wiki/Bishop_of_Grand_Rapids "Bishop of Grand Rapids").

The [Reformed Church in America](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_America "Reformed Church in America") (RCA) has about 154 congregations and 76,000 members mainly in Western Michigan,[[87]](#cite_note-88) heavily concentrated in the cities in Grand Rapids, Holland, and Zeeland. The denomination's main office is also in Grand Rapids.[[88]](#cite_note-89)
The Grand Rapids-Wyoming metropolitan area has 86 congregations with almost 49,000 members.
The [Protestant Reformed Churches in America](/wiki/Protestant_Reformed_Churches_in_America "Protestant Reformed Churches in America") (PRCA) traces its roots to the [First Protestant Reformed Church (Grand Rapids, Michigan)](/wiki/First_Protestant_Reformed_Church_(Grand_Rapids,_Michigan) "First Protestant Reformed Church (Grand Rapids, Michigan)") whose pastor was [Herman Hoeksema](/wiki/Herman_Hoeksema "Herman Hoeksema"), the founder of the church.[[89]](#cite_note-90) A majority of the PRCA's Classis East churches, about 13 congregations, are around Grand Rapids.[[90]](#cite_note-thearda.com-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93)

[![Grand Rapids aerial view in the 1930s](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Downtown_Grand_Rapids_from_the_air%2C_Grand_Rapids%2C_Michigan_%2864103%29.jpg/250px-Downtown_Grand_Rapids_from_the_air%2C_Grand_Rapids%2C_Michigan_%2864103%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Grand_Rapids_from_the_air,_Grand_Rapids,_Michigan_(64103).jpg)

Grand Rapids aerial view in the 1930s

The [United Reformed Churches in North America](/wiki/United_Reformed_Churches_in_North_America "United Reformed Churches in North America") has 12 congregations in Grand Rapids area; these congregations form the Classis of Michigan.[[93]](#cite_note-94) The Heritage Reformed Congregations' flagship and largest church is in Grand Rapids. The Netherlands Reformed Congregations in North America has 2 churches.[[94]](#cite_note-95) The [PC(USA)](/wiki/PC(USA) "PC(USA)") had 12 congregations and 7,000 members in the Grand Rapids-Wyoming Metropolitan statistical area, the [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ") had also 14 congregations and 5,400 members.[[90]](#cite_note-thearda.com-91)

The offices of the former [West Michigan Annual Conference](/wiki/West_Michigan_Annual_Conference "West Michigan Annual Conference") of the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") were in the East Hills Neighborhood of Grand Rapids. The West Michigan Annual Conference represented more than 400 local United Methodist churches in the western half of the lower peninsula with approximately 65,000 members in total.[[95]](#cite_note-96) In 2016, The West Michigan Conference Joined with the Detroit Annual Conference to form the Michigan Area Annual Conference. Grand Rapids is also home to the United Methodist Community House, whose mission is to increase the ability of children, youth, adults and families to succeed in a diverse community.[[96]](#cite_note-97) In 2010, The United Methodist Church had 61 congregations and 21,450 members in the Grand Rapids Metropolitan area.[[90]](#cite_note-thearda.com-91)

On October 2, 2022, [The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") announced a temple to be built in Grand Rapids.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Crime")]

The homicide rate in Grand Rapids was at its highest in the early 1990s, with the highest number of homicides being 34 in 1993.[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100) The average annual number of homicides in Grand Rapids between 2010 and 2020 was 12.4.[[100]](#cite_note-auto1-101) In 2014, Grand Rapids experienced the lowest homicide rate in fifty years, with six murders occurring that year.[[101]](#cite_note-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103)[[103]](#cite_note-104)

A collection of [Corewell Health](/wiki/Corewell_Health "Corewell Health") and [Michigan State University](/wiki/Michigan_State_University "Michigan State University") facilities on the [Medical Mile](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Medical_Mile "Grand Rapids Medical Mile")

Headquartered in Grand Rapids, [Corewell Health](/wiki/Corewell_Health "Corewell Health") (formerly Spectrum Health) is West Michigan's largest employer, with over 60,000 staff and 11,500 physicians in 2023.[[104]](#cite_note-about-105) Corewell Health's Meijer Heart Center, Lemmen-Holton Cancer Pavilion, and [Butterworth Hospital](/wiki/Butterworth_Hospital_(Michigan) "Butterworth Hospital (Michigan)"), a [level I trauma center](/wiki/Level_I_trauma_center "Level I trauma center"), are on the [Grand Rapids Medical Mile](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Medical_Mile "Grand Rapids Medical Mile"), which has world-class facilities that focus on the [health sciences](/wiki/Health_science "Health science"). They include the [Van Andel Research Institute](/wiki/Van_Andel_Institute#Van_Andel_Institute "Van Andel Institute"), [Grand Valley State University](/wiki/Grand_Valley_State_University "Grand Valley State University")'s Cook-DeVos Center for Health Sciences, and the [Michigan State University College of Human Medicine](/wiki/Michigan_State_University_College_of_Human_Medicine "Michigan State University College of Human Medicine") medical school's Secchia Center, along with [Ferris State University](/wiki/Ferris_State_University "Ferris State University")'s College of Pharmacy. Nearly a billion dollars has been invested in the Corewell Health Cancer Pavilion, the Corewell Health [Helen DeVos Children's Hospital](/wiki/Helen_DeVos_Children%27s_Hospital "Helen DeVos Children's Hospital"), and the expansion to the Van Andel Institute. These facilities have attracted many health science businesses to the area.

Grand Rapids has long been a center for manufacturing, dating back to its original roots in furniture manufacturing. Office furniture manufacturers such as [American Seating](/wiki/American_Seating "American Seating"), [Steelcase](/wiki/Steelcase "Steelcase") (and its subsidiaries [Coalesse](/wiki/Coalesse "Coalesse") and [Turnstone](/wiki/Turnstone_(company) "Turnstone (company)")), [Haworth](/wiki/Haworth_(company) "Haworth (company)"), and [Herman Miller](/wiki/Herman_Miller_(office_equipment) "Herman Miller (office equipment)") are based in and around the Grand Rapids area.[[105]](#cite_note-106)[[106]](#cite_note-107)[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-111) In 1881, the Furniture Manufacturers Association (FMA) was organized in Grand Rapids; making it the country's first furniture manufacturing advocacy group.[[111]](#cite_note-FMA-112) The Kindel Furniture Company[[112]](#cite_note-KF-113) and the [Hekman Furniture Company](/wiki/Howard_Miller_Clock_Company "Howard Miller Clock Company")[[113]](#cite_note-hekman-114) have been designing and manufacturing furniture in Grand Rapids since 1912 and 1922 respectively.

The Grand Rapids area is also known for its [automobile](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile") and aviation manufacturing industries, with [GE Aviation Systems](/wiki/GE_Aviation_Systems "GE Aviation Systems") having a location in the city.[[114]](#cite_note-115)

The Grand Rapids area is home to a number of well-known companies including [Alticor](/wiki/Alticor "Alticor")/[Amway](/wiki/Amway "Amway") (a [multi-level marketing](/wiki/Multi-level_marketing "Multi-level marketing") company), [Bissell](/wiki/Bissell "Bissell") (a privately owned vacuum cleaner and floor care product manufacturer), [SpartanNash](/wiki/SpartanNash "SpartanNash") (a food distributor and grocery store chain), [Foremost Insurance Company](/wiki/Farmers_Insurance_Group "Farmers Insurance Group") (a specialty lines insurance company), [Meijer](/wiki/Meijer "Meijer") (a regional [supercenter](/wiki/Supercenter "Supercenter") chain), GE Aviation (formerly [Smiths Industries](/wiki/Smiths_Industries "Smiths Industries"), an [aerospace](/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace") products company), [Wolverine World Wide](/wiki/Wolverine_World_Wide "Wolverine World Wide") (a designer and manufacturer of shoes, boots and clothing), [Universal Forest Products](/wiki/Universal_Forest_Products "Universal Forest Products") (a building materials company), and [Schuler Books & Music](/wiki/Schuler_Books_%26_Music "Schuler Books & Music"), one of the country's largest independent bookstores.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The city is known as a center of Christian publishing, home to [Zondervan](/wiki/Zondervan "Zondervan"), [Kregel Publications](/wiki/Kregel_Publications "Kregel Publications"), [Eerdmans Publishing](/wiki/William_B._Eerdmans_Publishing_Company "William B. Eerdmans Publishing Company") and [Our Daily Bread Ministries](/wiki/Our_Daily_Bread_Ministries "Our Daily Bread Ministries").

The city and its surrounding region house a successful food processing and agribusiness industry, which experienced a 10-year job growth rate of 45% from 2009 to 2019. The [Grand Rapids Downtown Market](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Downtown_Market "Grand Rapids Downtown Market"), opened in 2013, provides food education services, entrepreneurship guidance and serves as a [farmers market](/wiki/Farmers_market "Farmers market"). With Michigan being the second most agriculturally diverse state in the nation, the Greater Grand Rapids region is well known for its fruit production. Due to its proximity to [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"), the climate is considered especially prime for apple, peach, and blueberry farming. Greater Grand Rapids produces 1/3 of Michigan's total agricultural sales.

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Grand_Rapids_Independence_Day_2016.jpg/250px-Grand_Rapids_Independence_Day_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Rapids_Independence_Day_2016.jpg)

2016 [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(United_States) "Independence Day (United States)") celebration on the Grand River

In 1969, [Alexander Calder](/wiki/Alexander_Calder "Alexander Calder")'s abstract sculpture, *[La Grande Vitesse](/wiki/La_Grande_Vitesse "La Grande Vitesse")*, which translates from French as "the great swiftness" or more loosely as "grand rapids," was installed downtown on Vandenberg Plaza, the redesigned setting of Grand Rapids City Hall.[[115]](#cite_note-SculpturesitesGR.org-116) It was the first work of public art in the United States funded by the [National Endowment for the Arts](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Arts "National Endowment for the Arts").[[116]](#cite_note-endow1-117) The sculpture is informally known as "the Calder", and since its installation the city has hosted an annual *[Festival of the Arts](/wiki/Festival_of_the_Arts_(Grand_Rapids) "Festival of the Arts (Grand Rapids)")* in the area surrounding the sculpture, now known informally as "Calder Plaza".[[115]](#cite_note-SculpturesitesGR.org-116)[[117]](#cite_note-festarts-118) During the first weekend in June, several blocks of downtown surrounding the [Calder](/wiki/Alexander_Calder "Alexander Calder") stabile in [Vandenberg](/wiki/Arthur_H._Vandenberg "Arthur H. Vandenberg") Plaza are closed to traffic. The festival features several stages with free live performances, food booths selling a variety of ethnic cuisine, art demonstrations and sales, and other arts-related activities. Organizers bill it as the largest all-volunteer arts festival in the United States. Vandenberg Plaza also hosts various ethnic festivals throughout the summer season.

Each October, the city celebrates Polish culture, historically based on the West side of town, with [Pulaski Days](/wiki/Pulaski_Days "Pulaski Days").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Grand_Rapids_Public_Museum.jpg/250px-Grand_Rapids_Public_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Rapids_Public_Museum.jpg)

The [Grand Rapids Public Museum](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Public_Museum "Grand Rapids Public Museum") stretches along the Grand River.

In 1973, Grand Rapids hosted *Sculpture off the Pedestal*, an outdoor exhibition of public sculpture, which assembled works by 13 world-renowned artists, including [Mark di Suvero](/wiki/Mark_di_Suvero "Mark di Suvero"), [John Henry](/wiki/John_Raymond_Henry "John Raymond Henry"), [Kenneth Snelson](/wiki/Kenneth_Snelson "Kenneth Snelson"), [Robert Morris](/wiki/Robert_Morris_(artist) "Robert Morris (artist)"), [John Mason](/wiki/John_Mason_(artist) "John Mason (artist)"), [Lyman Kipp](/wiki/Lyman_Kipp "Lyman Kipp"), and [Stephen Antonakos](/wiki/Stephen_Antonakos "Stephen Antonakos"), in a single, citywide celebration. *Sculpture off the Pedestal* was a public/private partnership, including financial support by the [National Endowment for the Arts](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Arts "National Endowment for the Arts"), educational support from the Michigan Council for the Arts, and in-kind contributions from individuals, business, and industry. Fund-raising events, volunteers, and locals housing artists contributed to the public character of the event.

From 1980 to 2015, [Celebration on the Grand](/wiki/Celebration_on_the_Grand "Celebration on the Grand") was held the weekend after Labor Day, featuring free concerts, fireworks display and food booths. 'Celebration on the Grand' is an event that celebrates life in the Grand River valley.

On November 10, 2004, the grand [premiere](/wiki/Premiere "Premiere") of the film *[The Polar Express](/wiki/The_Polar_Express_(film) "The Polar Express (film)")* was held in Grand Rapids. It was adapted from the children's book by author and illustrator [Chris Van Allsburg](/wiki/Chris_Van_Allsburg "Chris Van Allsburg"), who lives in the city. His main character in the book (and movie) also lives in Grand Rapids, and the movie is briefly set in the city. The [Meijer Gardens](/wiki/Meijer_Gardens "Meijer Gardens") created a Polar Express display as part of their larger *[Christmas Around the World](/wiki/Frederik_Meijer_Gardens#Seasonal_events "Frederik Meijer Gardens")* exhibit.

In mid-2004, the [Grand Rapids Art Museum](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Art_Museum "Grand Rapids Art Museum") (GRAM) began construction of a new, larger building for its collection; it opened in October 2007 at 101 Monroe Center NW. The new building site faces the sculpture *Ecliptic,* by [Maya Lin](/wiki/Maya_Lin "Maya Lin"), at [Rosa Parks Circle](/wiki/Rosa_Parks_Circle "Rosa Parks Circle"). The museum was completed in 2007. It was the first new art museum to achieve gold-level [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design") certification by the [U.S. Green Building Council](/wiki/U.S._Green_Building_Council "U.S. Green Building Council").

[ArtPrize](/wiki/ArtPrize "ArtPrize"), the world's largest annual art competition determined by public voting, first took place in Grand Rapids from September 23 through October 10, 2009. This event was founded by Rick DeVos, grandson of Amway Corp. co-founder [Richard DeVos](/wiki/Richard_DeVos "Richard DeVos"), who offered $449,000 in cash prizes. A total of 1,262 artists exhibited their work for two weeks, and a total of 334,219 votes were cast. First prize, including a $250,000 cash prize, went to Brooklyn painter Ran Ortner.[[118]](#cite_note-artprizehistory-119) ArtPrize 2010 was held September 22 through October 10, 2010, with work by 1,713 artists on display. The first prize was awarded to Grand Rapids artist [Chris LaPorte](/w/index.php?title=Chris_LaPorte&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chris LaPorte (page does not exist)").[[119]](#cite_note-aboutartprize-120)
