### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/GRFord-Presidential.jpg/250px-GRFord-Presidential.jpg)](/wiki/File:GRFord-Presidential.jpg)

The [Gerald R. Ford Museum](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_Museum "Gerald R. Ford Museum"), dedicated in 1981, is located downtown on the west bank of the Grand River. [President Ford](/wiki/President_Ford "President Ford") and First Lady [Betty Ford](/wiki/Betty_Ford "Betty Ford") are buried on the museum's grounds.

Grand Rapids is the home of [John Ball Zoological Garden](/wiki/John_Ball_Zoological_Garden "John Ball Zoological Garden"), [Belknap Hill](/wiki/Belknap_Hill "Belknap Hill"), and the [Gerald R. Ford Museum](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_Museum "Gerald R. Ford Museum"). He and former First Lady Betty Ford were buried on the site. Significant buildings in the downtown include the [DeVos Place Convention Center](/wiki/DeVos_Place_Convention_Center "DeVos Place Convention Center"), [Van Andel Arena](/wiki/Van_Andel_Arena "Van Andel Arena"), the [Amway Grand Plaza Hotel](/wiki/Amway_Grand_Plaza_Hotel "Amway Grand Plaza Hotel"), and the [JW Marriott Hotel](/wiki/JW_Marriott_Grand_Rapids "JW Marriott Grand Rapids"). The [Urban Institute for Contemporary Arts](/w/index.php?title=Urban_Institute_for_Contemporary_Arts&action=edit&redlink=1 "Urban Institute for Contemporary Arts (page does not exist)") is downtown, and houses art exhibits, a movie theater, and the urban clay studio.[[120]](#cite_note-121)

Along the [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Michigan) "Grand River (Michigan)") are reconstructed earthwork [burial mounds](/wiki/Burial_mounds "Burial mounds"), which were constructed by the prehistoric [Hopewell tribe](/wiki/Hopewell_culture "Hopewell culture"); a [fish ladder](/wiki/Fish_ladder "Fish ladder"), and a riverwalk.

Grand Rapids is home to the [Van Andel Museum Center](/wiki/Public_Museum_of_Grand_Rapids "Public Museum of Grand Rapids"). Founded in 1854, it is among the oldest history museums in the United States. The museum's sites include its main building, constructed in 1994 on the west bank of the [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Michigan) "Grand River (Michigan)") (home to the [Roger B. Chaffee Planetarium](/wiki/Roger_B._Chaffee_Planetarium "Roger B. Chaffee Planetarium")); the Voigt House Victorian Museum, and the City Archives and Records Center. The latter held the museum and planetarium before 1994. Since the late 20th century, the museum has hosted notable exhibitions, including one on the [Dead Sea Scrolls](/wiki/Dead_Sea_Scrolls "Dead Sea Scrolls"), and *The Quest for Immortality: the Treasures of Ancient Egypt.* A non-profit institution, it is owned and managed by the Public Museum of Grand Rapids Foundation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Voight_House.jpg/250px-Voight_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Voight_House.jpg)

An example of [Châteauesque](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teauesque "Châteauesque") architecture in the [Heritage Hill Historic District](/wiki/Heritage_Hill_Historic_District_(Grand_Rapids,_Michigan) "Heritage Hill Historic District (Grand Rapids, Michigan)"), a neighborhood immediately east of downtown

[Heritage Hill](/wiki/Heritage_Hill_Historic_District_(Grand_Rapids,_Michigan) "Heritage Hill Historic District (Grand Rapids, Michigan)"), a neighborhood directly east of downtown, is one of the largest urban historic districts in the country. The first "neighborhood" of Grand Rapids, its 1,300 homes date from 1848 and represent more than 60 architectural styles. Of particular significance is the [Meyer May House](/wiki/Meyer_May_House "Meyer May House"), a [Prairie](/wiki/Prairie_School "Prairie School")-style home [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright") designed in 1908.[[121]](#cite_note-122) It was commissioned by local merchant Meyer May, who operated a men's clothing store (May's of Michigan).

The house is now owned and operated by [Steelcase Corporation](/wiki/Steelcase "Steelcase"). Steelcase manufactured the furniture for the [Johnson Wax Building](/wiki/Johnson_Wax_Building "Johnson Wax Building") in [Racine, Wisconsin](/wiki/Racine,_Wisconsin "Racine, Wisconsin"), which was also designed by Wright and is recognized as a landmark building. Because of those ties, Steelcase purchased and restored the property in the 1980s. The restoration has been heralded as one of the most accurate and complete of any Wright restoration. The home is used by Steelcase for special events and is open to the public for tours.

Grand Rapids' prominent craft beer culture has continued to garner the city national and international recognition in recent years, making it a destination for increasing numbers of tourists. The city was awarded the nation's "Best Beer City" for the third year in a row in 2023.[[122]](#cite_note-123)

### Entertainment and performing arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Entertainment and performing arts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Wealthy_Theatre_Grand_Rapids.jpg/250px-Wealthy_Theatre_Grand_Rapids.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wealthy_Theatre_Grand_Rapids.jpg)

[Wealthy Theatre](/wiki/Wealthy_Theatre "Wealthy Theatre")

Grand Rapids has several popular concert venues in which numerous bands have performed, including 20 Monroe Live, the DAAC, the Intersection, DeVos Performance Hall, Van Andel Arena, Royce Auditorium in St. Cecilia Music Center, Forest Hills Fine Arts Center, The Pyramid Scheme, and the Deltaplex, which was sold in 2023 for $5.5 million.

The Schubert Male Chorus of Grand Rapids was founded by Henry C. Post on November 19, 1883; the chorus continues to perform a variety of music.

The [Grand Rapids Symphony](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Symphony "Grand Rapids Symphony"), founded in 1930, is the largest performing arts organization in Grand Rapids with a roster of about 50 full-time and 30 part-time musicians. In addition to its own concert series, the orchestra under music director [Marcelo Lehninger](/wiki/Marcelo_Lehninger "Marcelo Lehninger") accompanies productions by Grand Rapids Ballet and Opera Grand Rapids, presenting more than 400 performances a year.[[123]](#cite_note-Symphony-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Grand_Rapids_Symphony_-_Marcelo_Lehninger.jpg/250px-Grand_Rapids_Symphony_-_Marcelo_Lehninger.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Rapids_Symphony_-_Marcelo_Lehninger.jpg)

[Grand Rapids Symphony](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Symphony "Grand Rapids Symphony")

The Grand Rapids Barbershop Chapter Great Lakes Chorus is an all-male *[a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella")* [barbershop](/wiki/Barbershop_music "Barbershop music") harmony chorus, including quartets. It is one of the oldest chapters in the Barbershop Harmony Society (formally known as the Society for the Preservation and Encouragement of Barber Shop Quartet Singing in America, or SPEBSQSA). The Grand Rapids chapter organized on November 1, 1939, for quartet singers; it is credited for holding the first society-sanctioned quartet contest in the "Michigan District" (now Pioneer District) in March 1941. In 1944 the Grand Rapids Chapter is credited with having the first International Quartet champions, "The Harmony Halls." In 1947 the Great Lakes Chorus (then called the Grand Rapids Chorus) was founded. In 1953 the first International Chorus Competition was held, and the Great Lakes Chorus took First Place, the first "International Convention Championship Chorus", under the direction of Robert Weaver.[[124]](#cite_note-GLC-125) The chorus is still very active as a non-profit singing for community, competition, and contracted performances.

Grand Rapids is home to many theaters and stages. The city's largest theater is [Meijer Majestic Theatre](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Civic_Theatre_%26_School_of_Theatre_Arts "Grand Rapids Civic Theatre & School of Theatre Arts") (renamed from Civic Theatre in 2006 after renovations to the original theater building were funded by private donations led by Fred and Lena Meijer); DeVos Hall, and the convertible Van Andel Arena. Further east of downtown is the historic [Wealthy Theatre](/wiki/Wealthy_Theatre "Wealthy Theatre"). Studio 28, the first [megaplex](/wiki/Megaplex "Megaplex") in the United States, is in Grand Rapids; it reopened in 1988 with a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of 6,000.[[125]](#cite_note-126) The megaplex ceased operations on November 23, 2008.[[126]](#cite_note-127)[[127]](#cite_note-128) The Grand Rapids company also owns many theaters around West Michigan. The [Acrisure Amphitheater](/wiki/Acrisure_Amphitheater "Acrisure Amphitheater"), a planned outdoor venue with 12,000 seats, is expected to open in 2026.[[128]](#cite_note-:0-129)

Grand Rapids Ballet Company was founded in 1971 and is one of Michigan's few professional ballet companies.[[129]](#cite_note-ballet-130) The ballet company is on Ellsworth Avenue in the [Heartside](/wiki/Heartside "Heartside") neighborhood, where it moved in 2000. In 2007, it expanded its facility by adding the LEED-certified Peter Wege Theater.[[129]](#cite_note-ballet-130)

Opera Grand Rapids, founded in 1966, is the state's longest-running professional company.[[130]](#cite_note-131) In February 2010, the opera moved into a new facility in the Fulton Heights neighborhood.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

Grand Rapids is also home to Art Prize, the largest art exposition in the U.S. Art Prize began in 2009 with the over 200,000 visitors and has since doubled the number of visitors it receives each year. Artprize receives many international visitors each year and is still growing with over 1,500 entries from 48 countries across 200+ venues in 2015.[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134)

[Van Andel Arena](/wiki/Van_Andel_Arena "Van Andel Arena"), a popular sports venue in Grand Rapids.

Grand Rapids is home to several professional and semi-professional sports teams. The [West Michigan Whitecaps](/wiki/West_Michigan_Whitecaps "West Michigan Whitecaps") of the [Midwest League](/wiki/Midwest_League "Midwest League") play at [LMCU Ballpark](/wiki/LMCU_Ballpark "LMCU Ballpark") and won the Championship Series six times (1996, 1998, 2004, 2006, 2007, 2015) and had the best regular-season record six times (1997, 1998, 2000, 2006, 2007, 2017). The Whitecaps are the Class High A affiliate of the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers"). The [Grand Rapids Griffins](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Griffins "Grand Rapids Griffins"), an ice hockey team of the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League"), play at the [Van Andel Arena](/wiki/Van_Andel_Arena "Van Andel Arena") and won the IHL [Fred A. Huber Trophy](/wiki/Fred_A._Huber_Trophy "Fred A. Huber Trophy") in 2001, and were AHL [Calder Cup](/wiki/Calder_Cup "Calder Cup") Champions in the 2012–2013 and 2016–2017 seasons. The Griffins are the AHL affiliate of the [Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings"). [Grand Rapids Gold](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Gold "Grand Rapids Gold") is an [NBA G League](/wiki/NBA_G_League "NBA G League") basketball team that plays at the [Van Andel Arena](/wiki/Van_Andel_Arena "Van Andel Arena"), with the team being an affiliate of the [Denver Nuggets](/wiki/Denver_Nuggets "Denver Nuggets"). The [Grand Rapids Rise](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Rise "Grand Rapids Rise") also play in the [Van Andel Arena](/wiki/Van_Andel_Arena "Van Andel Arena"), and are one of seven inaugural members of the [Pro Volleyball Federation (PVF)](/wiki/Pro_Volleyball_Federation "Pro Volleyball Federation"), a professional women's indoor volleyball league. The Vibe lost in the PVF Championship match to the [Omaha Supernovas](/wiki/Omaha_Supernovas "Omaha Supernovas") in May 2024.

[Grand Rapids FC](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_FC "Grand Rapids FC") was the city's highest-level soccer club from 2014 to 2021; the men's team played in the [National Premier Soccer League](/wiki/National_Premier_Soccer_League "National Premier Soccer League") for four seasons and moved to [USL League Two](/wiki/USL_League_Two "USL League Two") before folding.[[134]](#cite_note-135) The team averaged 4,509 spectators at [Houseman Field](/wiki/Houseman_Field "Houseman Field") during their inaugural season.[[135]](#cite_note-136) The women's team joined [United Women's Soccer](/wiki/United_Women%27s_Soccer "United Women's Soccer") in 2017 and was renamed to [Midwest United FC](/wiki/Midwest_United_FC "Midwest United FC") after it was acquired by a local soccer program in 2019. They won a national championship in the [2017 season](/wiki/2017_United_Women%27s_Soccer_season "2017 United Women's Soccer season"). In 2022, Midwest United moved to the [USL W League](/wiki/USL_W_League "USL W League") and established a men's team in USL League Two.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Amway Stadium](/wiki/Amway_Stadium "Amway Stadium"), planned to be built with 8,500 seats for a yet-unnamed professional soccer team, is scheduled to open in 2027.[[136]](#cite_note-137)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/WestMichigan_Whitecaps_DSCN8265.JPG/250px-WestMichigan_Whitecaps_DSCN8265.JPG)](/wiki/File:WestMichigan_Whitecaps_DSCN8265.JPG)

[LMCU Ballpark](/wiki/LMCU_Ballpark "LMCU Ballpark"), home of the [West Michigan Whitecaps](/wiki/West_Michigan_Whitecaps "West Michigan Whitecaps")

Former sports teams include the [Grand Rapids Danger](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Danger "Grand Rapids Danger"), [Grand Rapids Dragonfish](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Dragonfish "Grand Rapids Dragonfish"), [Grand Rapids Cyclones](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Cyclones "Grand Rapids Cyclones"), [Grand Rapids Tackers](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Tackers "Grand Rapids Tackers"), [Grand Rapids Rampage](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Rampage "Grand Rapids Rampage"), [Grand Rapids Hoops](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Hoops "Grand Rapids Hoops") ([Grand Rapids Mackers](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Mackers "Grand Rapids Mackers")), [Grand Rapids Flight](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Flight "Grand Rapids Flight"), [Grand Rapids Owls (1977–80)](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Owls_(1977%E2%80%9380) "Grand Rapids Owls (1977–80)"), [Grand Rapids Rockets](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Rockets "Grand Rapids Rockets"), [Grand Rapids Chicks](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Chicks "Grand Rapids Chicks"), [Grand Rapids Blazers](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Blazers "Grand Rapids Blazers") and the [Grand Rapids Shamrocks](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Shamrocks "Grand Rapids Shamrocks"). The Grand Rapids Blazers won the [United Football League](/wiki/United_Football_League_(1961%E2%80%9364) "United Football League (1961–64)") Championship in 1961.

Each year the [Amway River Bank Run](/wiki/Amway_River_Bank_Run "Amway River Bank Run") is held in downtown Grand Rapids. It draws participants from around the world; in 2010 there were over 22,000 participants. The [Grand Rapids Marathon](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Marathon "Grand Rapids Marathon") is held in downtown Grand Rapids in mid-October, usually on the same weekend as the [Detroit Marathon](/wiki/Detroit_Free_Press_Marathon "Detroit Free Press Marathon"). [Special Olympics](/wiki/Special_Olympics "Special Olympics") Michigan launched a campaign in 2021 to build a publicly funded $20 million facility called the Unified Sports and Inclusion Center that is destined to be the largest Special Olympics facility in the world.[[137]](#cite_note-138)

Amateur sporting organizations in the area include Grand Raggidy Roller Derby WFTDA league, Grand Rapids Rowing Association,[[138]](#cite_note-GRRA-139) Grand Rapids Rugby Club,[[139]](#cite_note-grrc-140) and the West Michigan Wheelchair Sports Association.[[140]](#cite_note-grwcsa-141) The West Michigan Sports Commission was the host organizing committee for the inaugural State Games of Michigan, held in Grand Rapids from June 25 to 27, 2010.[[141]](#cite_note-sgom-142)[[142]](#cite_note-Kaminski-143)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Calder_Plaza%2C_Grand_Rapids_City_Hall.jpg/250px-Calder_Plaza%2C_Grand_Rapids_City_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calder_Plaza,_Grand_Rapids_City_Hall.jpg)

Calder Plaza, where the Grand Rapids City Hall is located.

Under Michigan law, Grand Rapids is a [home rule city](/wiki/Home_Rule_Cities_Act_(Michigan) "Home Rule Cities Act (Michigan)") and adopted a city charter in 1916 providing for the [council-manager](/wiki/Council%E2%80%93manager_government "Council–manager government") form of municipal government.[[143]](#cite_note-GRgi-144)[[144]](#cite_note-grp-145) Under this system, the political responsibilities are divided between an elected City Commission, an elected City Comptroller and a hired full-time City Manager. The City Commission is made up of the mayor and two commissioners from each of three wards, for a total of seven commissioners. Each ward elects a commissioner to a four-year term every two years. The races—held in even-numbered years—are officially non-partisan, although the party and other political affiliations of candidates do sometimes come up during the campaign period. The Commission sets policy for the city, and is responsible for hiring the City Manager and other appointed officials. The elected City Comptroller verifies financial policies and budgets are followed and prepares the annual financial report.[[143]](#cite_note-GRgi-144) The city levies an income tax of 1.5 percent on residents and 0.75 percent on nonresidents.[[145]](#cite_note-146)

### Mayor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Mayor")]

See also: [List of mayors of Grand Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "List of mayors of Grand Rapids, Michigan")

The part-time mayor is elected every four years by the city at large and serves as chair of the commission, with a vote equal to a commissioner.[[143]](#cite_note-GRgi-144) In 2014, a [limit of two terms](/wiki/Term_limits "Term limits") was approved.[[146]](#cite_note-termlimits-147)

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Politics")]

The city proper and inner-suburbs favor the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), while outer-suburbs of Grand Rapids tend to support the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)").[[147]](#cite_note-NYT2019-148)[[148]](#cite_note-APpoli-149)

Traditionally, Grand Rapids has supported the Republican Party.[[147]](#cite_note-NYT2019-148)[[148]](#cite_note-APpoli-149) The city is located in the [3rd Congressional District](/wiki/Michigan%27s_3rd_congressional_district "Michigan's 3rd congressional district"), represented by [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [Hillary Scholten](/wiki/Hillary_Scholten "Hillary Scholten").[[149]](#cite_note-150) Former President [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford") represented the district (then numbered as the 5th) from 1949 to 1973 and is buried on the grounds of his [Presidential Museum](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_Presidential_Museum "Gerald R. Ford Presidential Museum") in Grand Rapids.

All four of the city's representatives in the [Michigan State House](/wiki/Michigan_State_House_of_Representatives "Michigan State House of Representatives") are Democrats. In the [Michigan Senate](/wiki/Michigan_Senate "Michigan Senate"), the city is represented by one Republican and one Democrat. The [30th State Senate District](/wiki/Michigan%27s_30th_Senate_district "Michigan's 30th Senate district"), which includes the northern half of the city, is represented by Republican [Mark Huizenga](/wiki/Mark_Huizenga "Mark Huizenga"). The [29th State Senate district](/wiki/Michigan%27s_29th_Senate_district "Michigan's 29th Senate district"), which includes the southern half of the city, was won by Democrat [Winnie Brinks](/wiki/Winnie_Brinks "Winnie Brinks") in 2018.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Grand_Rapids_Public_Library_2021.jpg/250px-Grand_Rapids_Public_Library_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Rapids_Public_Library_2021.jpg)

The Main Branch of the [Grand Rapids Public Library](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Public_Library "Grand Rapids Public Library"); the [Ryerson Building](/wiki/Ryerson_Library "Ryerson Library"), its oldest wing, opened in 1904

[K–12](/wiki/K%E2%80%9312 "K–12") public education is provided by the [Grand Rapids Public Schools](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Public_Schools "Grand Rapids Public Schools") (GRPS) as well as a number of [charter schools](/wiki/Charter_schools "Charter schools"). [City High-Middle School](/wiki/City_High-Middle_School "City High-Middle School"), a magnet school for academically talented students in the metropolitan region operated by GRPS.[[150]](#cite_note-151) Grand Rapids is also home to the oldest co-educational Catholic high school in the United States, [Catholic Central High School](/wiki/Catholic_Central_High_School_(Grand_Rapids,_Michigan) "Catholic Central High School (Grand Rapids, Michigan)").[[151]](#cite_note-psr-152) [National Heritage Academies](/wiki/National_Heritage_Academies "National Heritage Academies"), which operates charter schools across several states, has its headquarters in Grand Rapids.[[152]](#cite_note-153)

Grand Rapids is home to several colleges and universities. The private, religious schools: [Aquinas College](/wiki/Aquinas_College_Grand_Rapids "Aquinas College Grand Rapids"), [Calvin University](/wiki/Calvin_University "Calvin University"), [Cornerstone University](/wiki/Cornerstone_University "Cornerstone University"), [Grace Christian University](/wiki/Grace_Christian_University "Grace Christian University"), and [Kuyper College](/wiki/Kuyper_College "Kuyper College"), each have a campus within the city. The seminaries [Calvin Theological Seminary](/wiki/Calvin_Theological_Seminary "Calvin Theological Seminary"), [Grand Rapids Theological Seminary](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Theological_Seminary "Grand Rapids Theological Seminary"), and [Puritan Reformed Theological Seminary](/wiki/Puritan_Reformed_Theological_Seminary "Puritan Reformed Theological Seminary") are in Grand Rapids. [Northwood University](/wiki/Northwood_University "Northwood University"), a private university with its main campus in [Midland, Michigan](/wiki/Midland,_Michigan "Midland, Michigan"), has a satellite campus downtown near the "medical mile". [Davenport University](/wiki/Davenport_University "Davenport University"), a private, non-profit, multi-location university with 14 campuses statewide, has its main campus just outside Grand Rapids.

As for public [tertiary institutions](/wiki/Tertiary_institution "Tertiary institution"), [Grand Rapids Community College](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Community_College "Grand Rapids Community College") (GRCC) maintains a campus downtown and facilities in other parts of the city and surrounding region.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/GVSU_Cook_Devos.jpg/250px-GVSU_Cook_Devos.jpg)](/wiki/File:GVSU_Cook_Devos.jpg)

The Cook-DeVos Center for Health Sciences, on Grand Rapids' "Medical Mile", is part of [Grand Valley State University](/wiki/Grand_Valley_State_University "Grand Valley State University")'s Pew Grand Rapids campus[[153]](#cite_note-campus-154)

[Grand Valley State University](/wiki/Grand_Valley_State_University "Grand Valley State University"), with its main campus in nearby [Allendale](/wiki/Allendale,_Michigan "Allendale, Michigan"), continues to develop its presence downtown by expanding its Pew Campus, begun in the 1980s on the west bank of the Grand River.[[153]](#cite_note-campus-154) This downtown campus covers 67 acres (27 ha) in two locations and is home to 12 buildings and three leased spaces.[[154]](#cite_note-155) Into the 2000s, Grand Valley State University expanded its medical education programs into Medical Mile, constructing various facilities such as the Cook-DeVos Center for Health Sciences in 2003.[[155]](#cite_note-156) The university expanded across [I-196](/wiki/I-196 "I-196") from the Medical Mile into the [Belknap Lookout](/wiki/Belknap_Lookout "Belknap Lookout") neighborhood in the 2010s, constructing the Raleigh Finkelstein Hall to assist with medical and nursing studies.[[156]](#cite_note-157)

[Ferris State University](/wiki/Ferris_State_University "Ferris State University") has a growing campus downtown, including the Applied Technology Center (operated with GRCC) and the [Kendall College of Art and Design](/wiki/Kendall_College_of_Art_and_Design "Kendall College of Art and Design"), a formerly private institution that now is part of Ferris. Ferris State also has a branch of the College of Pharmacy downtown on the medical mile. [Western Michigan University](/wiki/Western_Michigan_University "Western Michigan University") has a long-standing graduate program in the city, with facilities downtown, and in the southeast. The Van Andel Institute, a cancer research institute established in 1996, also resides on the medical mile; the institute established a graduate school in 2005 to train Ph.D. students in cellular, genetic, and molecular biology.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Calvin_aerial.jpg/250px-Calvin_aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calvin_aerial.jpg)

[Calvin University](/wiki/Calvin_University "Calvin University"), a Christian [private university](/wiki/Private_university "Private university")

Grand Rapids is home to the Secchia Center medical education building, a $90 million, seven-story, 180,000-square-foot (17,000 m2) facility, at Michigan Street and Division Avenue, part of the [Grand Rapids Medical Mile](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Medical_Mile "Grand Rapids Medical Mile"). The building is home to the Grand Rapids Campus of the [Michigan State University](/wiki/Michigan_State_University "Michigan State University") College of Human Medicine. This campus trains medical students through all four years of their medical education. The state-of-the-art facility includes clinical examination rooms, simulation suites, classrooms, offices, and student areas.[[157]](#cite_note-http://news.msu.edu/story/8262/-158)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media in Grand Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/Media_in_Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Media in Grand Rapids, Michigan")

The *[Grand Rapids Press](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Press "Grand Rapids Press")* is a daily newspaper, while [Advance Newspapers](/wiki/Advance_Newspapers "Advance Newspapers") publishes a group of weekly papers that provide community-based news. [Gemini Media](/wiki/Gemini_Media "Gemini Media") is a niche, regional publishing company that produces the weekly newspaper *[Grand Rapids Business Journal](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Business_Journal "Grand Rapids Business Journal")*; the magazines *Grand Rapids Magazine*, *Grand Rapids Family* and *Michigan Blue*; and several other quarterly and annual business-to-business publications. *[El Vocero Hispano](/wiki/El_Vocero_Hispano "El Vocero Hispano")* publishes West Michigan's largest Spanish language newspaper for the Latino community.[[158]](#cite_note-:23-159) Two free monthly entertainment guides are distributed: *REVUE,*[[159]](#cite_note-160) which covers music and the arts, and *RECOIL,* which covers music and offers *[Onion](/wiki/The_Onion "The Onion")*-style satire. *[The Rapidian](/w/index.php?title=The_Rapidian&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Rapidian (page does not exist)")* is an online-based citizen journalism project funded by grants from the [Knight Foundation](/wiki/Knight_Foundation "Knight Foundation") and local community foundations.[[160]](#cite_note-Rapidian-161) It is reprinted or cited by other local media outlets.[[161]](#cite_note-Rapidian_Reprinted-162)

Grand Rapids, combined with nearby [Kalamazoo](/wiki/Kalamazoo,_Michigan "Kalamazoo, Michigan") and [Battle Creek](/wiki/Battle_Creek,_Michigan "Battle Creek, Michigan"), was ranked in 2019 as the 45th-largest television market in the U.S. by [Nielsen Media Research](/wiki/Nielsen_Media_Research "Nielsen Media Research").[[162]](#cite_note-Nielsen-163) The market is served by stations including: [WOOD-TV](/wiki/WOOD-TV "WOOD-TV") (channel 8, [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")), [WZZM-TV](/wiki/WZZM-TV "WZZM-TV") (channel 13, [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company")), [WXMI](/wiki/WXMI "WXMI") (channel 17, [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company")), and [WXSP-CD](/wiki/WXSP-CD "WXSP-CD") (channel 15, [MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV")); Battle Creek-based [WOTV](/wiki/WOTV "WOTV") (channel 41, [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") and [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW") on DT2) and [WZPX-TV](/wiki/WZPX-TV "WZPX-TV") (channel 43, [Ion](/wiki/Ion_Television "Ion Television")); Kalamazoo-based [WWMT](/wiki/WWMT "WWMT") (channel 3, [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")) and [WLLA](/wiki/WLLA "WLLA") (channel 64, Independent); and [Muskegon](/wiki/Muskegon,_Michigan "Muskegon, Michigan")-based [WTLJ](/wiki/WTLJ "WTLJ") (channel 54, [TCT](/wiki/Tri-State_Christian_Television "Tri-State Christian Television")); along with other surrounding stations. [WGVU-TV](/wiki/WGVU-TV "WGVU-TV") is the area's [PBS](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Service "Public Broadcasting Service") member station.

The Grand Rapids area is served by 16 [AM](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting") radio stations and 28 [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") stations.[[163]](#cite_note-Radio-164)

### Mass transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Mass transit")]

#### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Bus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Rapid_SilverLine.jpg/250px-Rapid_SilverLine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rapid_SilverLine.jpg)

[SilverLine](/wiki/Silver_Line_(Grand_Rapids) "Silver Line (Grand Rapids)") [BRT](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") bus at the [Rapid Central Station](/wiki/Rapid_Central_Station "Rapid Central Station")

The [Interurban Transit Partnership](/wiki/Interurban_Transit_Partnership "Interurban Transit Partnership"), which brands itself as *The Rapid*, provides public bus transportation. Transportation is also provided by the DASH buses: the "Downtown Area Shuttle." DASH bus rides are free.[[167]](#cite_note-168) These provide transportation to and from the parking lots in the city of Grand Rapids to designated loading and unloading spots around the city. The area's Greyhound Bus terminal is integrated into the Central Station of the Rapid, simplifying transfers between Greyhound and local buses.

[Indian Trails](/wiki/Indian_Trails "Indian Trails") provides daily intercity bus service of varying frequencies between Grand Rapids and [Petoskey, Michigan](/wiki/Petoskey,_Michigan "Petoskey, Michigan"),[[168]](#cite_note-169) between Grand Rapids and [Benton Harbor, Michigan](/wiki/Benton_Harbor,_Michigan "Benton Harbor, Michigan"),[[169]](#cite_note-170) and between Grand Rapids and [Kalamazoo, Michigan](/wiki/Kalamazoo,_Michigan "Kalamazoo, Michigan")[[170]](#cite_note-171) with intermediate stops.

In August 2014, the [SilverLine](/wiki/Silver_Line_(Grand_Rapids) "Silver Line (Grand Rapids)") opened, Michigan's first [bus rapid transit](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") line, an express bus line designed to function like a light rail system.[[171]](#cite_note-172) There are plans in the works to add more express routes, secondary stations, a streetcar and dedicated (exclusive) highway lanes.[[172]](#cite_note-173)

#### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Air")]

Commercial air service to Grand Rapids is provided by [Gerald R. Ford International Airport](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_International_Airport "Gerald R. Ford International Airport") (GRR). Eight passenger airlines and two cargo airlines operate over 150 daily flights to 34 nonstop destinations across the United States. International service was formerly operated to Toronto, Canada by Air Canada Express. The airport was formerly named Kent County International Airport before gaining its present name in 1999.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The first regularly scheduled air service in the United States was between Grand Rapids and Detroit (actually Dearborn's Ford Airport) on a Ford-Stout monoplane named Miss Grand Rapids, which began on July 26, 1926. [Delta Air Lines](/wiki/Delta_Air_Lines "Delta Air Lines") continues to operate this route today to their hub at [Detroit Metropolitan Wayne County Airport](/wiki/Detroit_Metropolitan_Wayne_County_Airport "Detroit Metropolitan Wayne County Airport") (DTW).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Rapids,_Michigan&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Amtrak_Grand_Rapids.jpg/250px-Amtrak_Grand_Rapids.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amtrak_Grand_Rapids.jpg)

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") train at Grand Rapids station

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") provides direct train service to Chicago from the [passenger station](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_(Amtrak_station) "Grand Rapids (Amtrak station)") via the *[Pere Marquette](/wiki/Pere_Marquette_(Amtrak_train) "Pere Marquette (Amtrak train)")* line.[[173]](#cite_note-174)[[174]](#cite_note-PMHS-175) Freight service is provided by [CSX](/wiki/CSX "CSX"), the [Grand Elk Railroad](/wiki/Grand_Elk_Railroad "Grand Elk Railroad"), [Marquette Rail](/wiki/Marquette_Rail "Marquette Rail"), and the [Grand Rapids Eastern Railroad](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Eastern_Railroad "Grand Rapids Eastern Railroad").
